verses.text: After three years, God sends Elijah to show himself to Ahab, promising rain. The famine is at its worst, yet the king's first concern is grass for his horses. A heart far from God worries over cattle while ignoring the God whose hand has shut the heavens.

After three years, God sends Elijah to show himself to Ahab, promising rain. The famine is at its worst, yet the king's first concern is grass for his horses. A heart far from God worries over cattle while ignoring the God whose hand has shut the heavens.

After three years, God sends Elijah to show himself to Ahab, promising rain. The famine is at its worst, yet the king's first concern is grass for his horses. A heart far from God worries over cattle while ignoring the God whose hand has shut the heavens.

We learn that Obadiah, Ahab's God-fearing steward, had hidden a hundred prophets from Jezebel and fed them. Even in the darkest court, God keeps a faithful servant. His people are often planted quietly in unlikely places, doing brave good where no one would expect it.

We learn that Obadiah, Ahab's God-fearing steward, had hidden a hundred prophets from Jezebel and fed them. Even in the darkest court, God keeps a faithful servant. His people are often planted quietly in unlikely places, doing brave good where no one would expect it.

We learn that Obadiah, Ahab's God-fearing steward, had hidden a hundred prophets from Jezebel and fed them. Even in the darkest court, God keeps a faithful servant. His people are often planted quietly in unlikely places, doing brave good where no one would expect it.

Elijah meets Obadiah and bids him announce his coming to Ahab, but Obadiah fears the prophet will vanish and cost him his life. His faith is real but timid. Even sincere believers can be gripped by fear that makes them hesitate to do what God plainly asks.

Obadiah recounts his loyalty and his dread, and Elijah swears he will surely appear to Ahab that day. The bold prophet steadies the anxious servant with a sure word. God's clear promise is the cure for the fears that make obedience feel impossible.

Ahab greets Elijah, 'Art thou he that troubleth Israel?' Elijah throws it back: it is Ahab and his idols who troubled it. He summons all Israel and Baal's prophets to Carmel. The faithful are often blamed for the trouble that the wicked themselves have caused.

Elijah challenges Israel: 'How long halt ye between two opinions?' Let the God who answers by fire be God. The people answer nothing. Wavering between God and idols is itself a verdict; a heart that will not decide for the LORD has already drifted from Him.

Baal's prophets cry, leap, and cut themselves from morning to evening, but 'there was no voice, nor any that answered.' Elijah mocks — perhaps their god is asleep. False gods cannot hear or help; all the frenzy in the world cannot make an idol answer.

Elijah repairs the broken altar of the LORD with twelve stones for the twelve tribes. Before the fire, he restores true worship. He will not improvise; he rebuilds on the old foundation God appointed, refusing to let Israel's brokenness be forgotten.

He arranges the sacrifice and drenches it with water three times till the trench is full — leaving no room for trickery. He makes the miracle humanly impossible. Faith does not fear to remove every excuse, that the answer may be unmistakably God's.

He arranges the sacrifice and drenches it with water three times till the trench is full — leaving no room for trickery. He makes the miracle humanly impossible. Faith does not fear to remove every excuse, that the answer may be unmistakably God's.

Elijah prays simply: let it be known thou art God, that thou hast turned their hearts back. No frenzy, just a quiet plea for God's glory and His people's return. The contrast with Baal's prophets is total — true prayer is calm, brief, and aimed at God's honour.

Elijah prays simply: let it be known thou art God, that thou hast turned their hearts back. No frenzy, just a quiet plea for God's glory and His people's return. The contrast with Baal's prophets is total — true prayer is calm, brief, and aimed at God's honour.

Fire falls and consumes the sacrifice, the wood, the stones, the dust, and even the water, and the people fall on their faces: 'The LORD, he is the God.' The answer is overwhelming and complete. When God acts, He leaves no doubt who alone deserves worship.

Fire falls and consumes the sacrifice, the wood, the stones, the dust, and even the water, and the people fall on their faces: 'The LORD, he is the God.' The answer is overwhelming and complete. When God acts, He leaves no doubt who alone deserves worship.

Elijah has the prophets of Baal seized and slain at the brook, executing God's law against those who led Israel into idolatry. The day of decision is also a day of judgment. Tolerated false worship, once exposed, must finally be put away, not left to deceive again.

Elijah tells Ahab rain is coming, then bows to the ground and sends his servant seven times to look toward the sea. The man who called down fire still labours in persistent prayer. Even the answer already promised is sought with earnest, repeated petition.

Elijah tells Ahab rain is coming, then bows to the ground and sends his servant seven times to look toward the sea. The man who called down fire still labours in persistent prayer. Even the answer already promised is sought with earnest, repeated petition.

Elijah tells Ahab rain is coming, then bows to the ground and sends his servant seven times to look toward the sea. The man who called down fire still labours in persistent prayer. Even the answer already promised is sought with earnest, repeated petition.

At last a little cloud rises, the sky blackens, and a great rain falls, and Elijah runs ahead of Ahab's chariot by the power of God. The drought breaks; mercy returns. When His people turn back, God opens heaven again and pours out long-withheld blessing.
